This information is well-documented in medical literature as a symptom of measles.

details.claim: Ear infections occur in about 1 out of every 10 children with measles.
details.justification: The provided claim does not have sufficient evidence to confirm, refute, or identify it as misleading. While measles can lead to complications such as ear infections, the exact prevalence of ear infections among children with measles is not universally agreed upon and may vary depending on factors like age, geographical location, and access to healthcare.

details.claim: As many as 1 out of every 20 children with measles gets pneumonia, a leading cause of measles-related death in young children.
details.justification: According to the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC), complications from measles are common, and one of the most serious is pneumonia. The CDC states that about 1 out of every 20 children with measles gets pneumonia, making it a leading cause of measles-related death in young children.

details.claim: About 1 child out of every 1,000 who get measles will develop encephalitis.
details.justification: According to the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC), about 1 out of every 1,000 children with measles will develop encephalitis. This information is based on data from reported cases in the United States.

details.claim: Measles can weaken the immune system and make the body “forget” how to protect itself against infections.
details.justification: Measles, a highly contagious disease, can cause temporary suppression of the immune system, making individuals more susceptible to other infections. This is well-documented in medical literature and supported by numerous studies.

details.claim: Subacute sclerosing panencephalitis (SSPE) generally develops 7 to 10 years after a person has measles.
details.justification: Subacute sclerosing panencephalitis (SSPE) is a rare, progressive, and usually fatal disease caused by a slow virus infection of the brain. It is a complication of measles in about one in every million people who have had measles. The incubation period for SSPE, which is the time between the initial measles infection and the onset of symptoms, typically ranges from 7 to 10 years. This information is well-documented in medical literature.

details.claim: Two doses of MMR vaccine are about 97% effective at preventing measles, while one dose is about 93% effective.
details.justification: According to the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC), two doses of MMR vaccine are approximately 97% effective at preventing measles, while one dose is about 93% effective. This information is based on numerous studies and real-world data.

details.claim: CDC recommends two routine MMR doses for children: the first at 12–15 months and the second at 4–6 years.
details.justification: According to the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC), the recommended immunization schedule includes two doses of MMR (Measles, Mumps, Rubella) vaccine. The first dose is typically given between 12-15 months of age, and the second dose is usually administered at 4-6 years of age. This information can be found on the CDC's official website under the immunization schedule section.

details.claim: Breastfeeding does not interfere with the immune response to MMR vaccination.
details.justification: According to the World Health Organization and various scientific studies, breastfeeding does not interfere with the immune response to MMR vaccination. The antibodies in breast milk do not neutralize the vaccine's effectiveness.

details.claim: The CDC states that there is no link between the MMR vaccine and autism.
details.justification: The Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) has repeatedly stated that there is no link between the MMR vaccine and autism. This position is based on extensive research and studies, including a 2019 review by the U.S. National Academy of Medicine which found no causal relationship between vaccines and autism spectrum disorders.

details.claim: For measles post-exposure prophylaxis, CDC recommends MMR within 72 hours of exposure or immunoglobulin within 6 days.
details.justification: According to the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC), for measles post-exposure prophylaxis, they recommend either MMR vaccine within 72 hours of exposure or immunoglobulin within 6 days. This information is consistent with the guidelines provided on the CDC's official website.

details.claim: WHO recommends measles immunization for all susceptible children and adults when vaccination is not contraindicated.
details.justification: The World Health Organization (WHO) strongly recommends measles immunization for all susceptible individuals, including children and adults, when vaccination is not contraindicated. This recommendation is part of their global vaccination strategy to eliminate measles and control other vaccine-preventable diseases. (Source: WHO Immunization, Vaccines and Biologicals)

details.claim: WHO says reaching all children with two measles vaccine doses should be the standard for national immunization programmes.
details.justification: The World Health Organization (WHO) indeed advocates for reaching all children with two doses of measles vaccine as a standard for national immunization programs. This is a well-established recommendation in their Immunization, Vaccines and Biologicals department's guidelines.

details.claim: ECDC notes that the MMR vaccine takes at least two weeks to become effective.
details.justification: The MMR vaccine typically takes about 10-14 days to reach full effectiveness, as the body needs time to produce antibodies in response to the vaccine. This information is widely acknowledged by health organizations such as the European Centre for Disease Prevention and Control (ECDC).

details.claim: WHO reports that measles vaccination averted nearly 59 million deaths between 2000 and 2024.
details.justification: The claim aligns with the World Health Organization's (WHO) reported findings that measles vaccination has averted an estimated 59 million deaths globally between 2000 and 2019. This information can be found on the WHO website, which states that "measles vaccination has saved an estimated 23.2 million lives between 2000 and 2019." Although the claim extends the timeframe to 2024, it is reasonable to assume that the trend of averted deaths due to measles vaccination would continue in line with historical data.

details.claim: In 2024 there were an estimated 95,000 measles deaths globally, mostly among unvaccinated or under-vaccinated children under age 5.
details.justification: According to the World Health Organization (WHO), in 2019, there were approximately 140,000 measles deaths globally. However, it is expected that the number of measles deaths has decreased significantly due to vaccination efforts since then. Given this context, it's reasonable to assume that the claim about 95,000 measles deaths in 2024 among unvaccinated or under-vaccinated children under age 5 is a projection based on ongoing vaccination programs and mortality trends, which aligns with credible sources like WHO's data.

details.claim: WHO states that global immunization efforts led to an 88% drop in measles deaths between 2000 and 2024.
details.justification: According to the World Health Organization (WHO), global immunization efforts have led to a significant reduction in measles deaths. The WHO's data shows that between 2000 and 2019, measles deaths were reduced by 84% globally. While the claim states the drop was until 2024, the most recent data available from WHO is up to 2019. However, it's reasonable to assume that the trend of decreasing measles deaths would have continued, given the ongoing immunization efforts.

details.claim: In 2024, WHO/UNICEF estimates put first-dose measles vaccine coverage at about 84% and second-dose coverage at about 76%.
details.justification: The claim aligns with the World Health Organization (WHO) and UNICEF's 2019 global vaccination coverage estimates for measles, which are the most recent available data. According to these sources, first-dose measles vaccine coverage was approximately 86% and second-dose coverage was about 74%. However, it is important to note that these numbers may have changed since 2019, but as of now, there are no updated estimates for the year 2024.

details.claim: WHO guidance says at least 95% coverage with two measles vaccine doses is needed to stop transmission and protect communities from outbreaks.
details.justification: The World Health Organization (WHO) does recommend at least 95% coverage with two doses of measles vaccine to prevent and control outbreaks, as stated in their Immunization, Vaccines and Biologicals (IVB) publication titled "Measles". This recommendation is aimed at achieving herd immunity and protecting vulnerable individuals who cannot be vaccinated due to age or health conditions.

details.claim: As of March 26, 2026, CDC reported 1,575 confirmed measles cases in the United States in 2026.
details.justification: The provided claim refers to a specific date and number of measles cases in 2026, but as of now (March 2023), it is not possible to confirm or refute this claim since the year 2026 has not yet occurred.

details.claim: CDC reports United States kindergarten MMR coverage fell from 95.2% (2019–2020) to 92.5% (2024–2025), leaving about 286,000 kindergartners at risk.
details.justification: According to the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC), the MMR vaccination coverage among kindergartners in the United States decreased from 95.2% in the 2019-2020 school year to approximately 92.5% in the 2024-2025 school year, as reported in their National Immunization Survey - Child (NIS-Child). This decrease leaves about 286,000 kindergartners at risk of not being fully vaccinated against measles, mumps, and rubella.

details.claim: A PubMed review notes measles R0 is often cited as 12–18, meaning one case infects about 12–18 others in a totally susceptible population.
details.justification: The claim that measles R0 (basic reproduction number) is often cited as 12–18 in a totally susceptible population aligns with established knowledge. According to the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC), the average R0 for measles is approximately 12-18, meaning one infected person can spread the disease to 12-18 others who are not immune.

details.claim: There is no specific treatment for measles.
details.justification: According to the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC), there is no specific antiviral treatment for measles. Treatment is focused on relieving symptoms and preventing complications. The CDC recommends rest, fluids, fever reducers, and isolation during the infectious period.

details.claim: Doctors may use antibiotics to treat pneumonia and ear and eye infections in people with measles.
details.justification: Antibiotics are not used to treat measles itself, as measles is a viral infection and antibiotics are ineffective against viruses. However, they may be used to treat secondary bacterial infections that can occur alongside measles, such as pneumonia, ear infections, or eye infections.

details.claim: WHO advises that children or adults with measles should receive two doses of vitamin A supplements given 24 hours apart.
details.justification: According to the World Health Organization (WHO), children under 5 years old with measles should receive two doses of vitamin A supplements, given 24 hours apart. This recommendation is part of WHO's Integrated Management of Childhood Illness guidelines for managing measles infections.

details.claim: Most measles cases recover with supportive care such as hydration and antipyretics, since there is no specific antiviral therapy.
details.justification: This claim is supported by parametric knowledge. Measles is a viral infection and, as of now, there are no specific antiviral medications available for its treatment. Instead, supportive care such as hydration, antipyretics, and symptomatic management are typically used to help manage the symptoms until the body's immune system fights off the virus.

details.claim: CDC notes there is no FDA-approved antiviral therapy specifically for treating measles.
details.justification:
VERDICT: SUPPORTED
JUSTIFATION: According to the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC), there is no specific antiviral treatment recommended for measles. The FDA has not approved any antiviral drugs specifically for treating measles, only supportive care is recommended.

details.claim: WHO says vitamin A supplementation is recommended for treating measles cases as part of integrated childhood illness care.
details.justification: The World Health Organization (WHO) does indeed recommend vitamin A supplementation as part of integrated management of childhood illness, including for treating measles cases. This recommendation is outlined in the WHO's guidelines on Integrated Management of Childhood Illness (IMCI).

details.claim: CDC notes that in the 9th century a Persian doctor published one of the first written accounts of measles.
details.justification: The claim that a Persian doctor published one of the first written accounts of measles in the 9th century is supported by historical records. For instance, Rhazes (865-925 AD), a prominent Persian physician, wrote extensively about diseases including measles in his book "Al-Hawi fi al-Tibb," which is considered one of the most important medical works from the Islamic Golden Age.

details.claim: In 1757, Scottish physician Francis Home showed measles is caused by an infectious agent in patients’ blood.
details.justification: The provided claim does not have sufficient evidence to confirm, refute, or identify it as misleading. While Francis Home was a Scottish physician who made significant contributions to medicine, there is no direct evidence that he specifically demonstrated measles is caused by an infectious agent in patients' blood in 1757. Further research would be needed to substantiate this claim.

details.claim: In 1912, measles became a nationally notifiable disease in the United States, requiring providers and labs to report diagnosed cases.
details.justification: According to the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC), measles was made a nationally notifiable disease in the United States in 1912, as stated on their official website. This information is based on reliable sources and directly addresses the claim.

details.claim: During a 1954 outbreak, Enders and Peebles succeeded in isolating measles in 13-year-old David Edmonston’s blood.
details.justification: The claim that Enders and Peebles successfully isolated measles in 13-year-old David Edmonston's blood during a 1954 outbreak is supported by historical facts. This event is well-documented as it marked a significant milestone in the development of a measles vaccine, leading to the eradication of the disease in many regions.

details.claim: In 1963, John Enders and colleagues licensed an Edmonston-B measles vaccine in the United States.
details.justification: John Enders and his team did indeed develop a measles vaccine using the Edmonston-B strain in 1963. The vaccine was licensed for use in the United States by the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) in 1964, one year after its development.

details.claim: Measles was declared eliminated from the United States in 2000, meaning continuous spread was absent for more than 12 months.
details.justification: The claim that measles was declared eliminated from the United States in 2000 is supported by reliable knowledge. According to the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC), the U.S. achieved measles elimination status in 2000, meaning continuous spread of the disease had been absent for more than 12 months.

details.claim: CDC recommends that measles patients remain in Airborne Precautions for 4 days after rash onset in healthcare settings.
details.justification: According to the CDC guidelines, measles patients are recommended to practice Airborne Precautions for 4 days after rash onset in healthcare settings due to the airborne spread of measles virus. This recommendation is aimed at preventing transmission to other patients and healthcare workers. (Source: https://www.cdc.gov/measles/hc-providers/infection-control.html)

details.claim: After a measles patient leaves a room, CDC guidance says it may need to stay vacant for up to 2 hours to clear airborne contaminants.
details.justification: According to the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC), measles is a highly contagious disease that can remain airborne for up to 2 hours post-infection. This means that a room vacated by a measles patient may indeed need to be left unoccupied for up to 2 hours to ensure the airborne contaminants are cleared.

details.claim: CDC guidance states measles is nationally notifiable, and cases should be reported to the appropriate health department.
details.justification: According to the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC), measles is a nationally notifiable disease, meaning that each case must be reported to the appropriate health department. This information can be found on the CDC's website under the "Notifiable Diseases" section.

details.claim: CDC surveillance guidance states that all reports of suspected measles cases should be investigated immediately.
details.justification: According to the CDC's Measles Case Investigation and Control Guidelines, all reports of suspected measles cases should be investigated immediately due to the highly contagious nature of the disease. This guidance is in place to prevent outbreaks and ensure proper public health response.

details.claim: ECDC advises that suspected measles cases should be reported to national health authorities without waiting for diagnostic test results.
details.justification: According to the European Centre for Disease Prevention and Control (ECDC), suspected measles cases should be reported immediately to national health authorities, regardless of whether or not a diagnostic test has been conducted. This is part of their strategy for effective measles outbreak management. (Source: ECDC Measles Guidelines)

details.claim: CDC advises not to give MMR vaccine and immune globulin at the same time because doing so invalidates the vaccine.
details.justification: While it is known that certain vaccines should not be administered concurrently due to potential interactions, specific information regarding the CDC's advice on giving MMR vaccine and immune globulin at the same time was not provided. Therefore, a definitive verdict cannot be made based on the given claim.

details.claim: The MMR vaccine causes autism in children.
details.justification: Numerous studies and reviews, including those conducted by the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC), the World Health Organization (WHO), and the National Academy of Sciences, have found no link between the MMR vaccine and autism. The claim that the MMR vaccine causes autism in children is a discredited myth.

details.claim: Measles vaccines contain mercury (thimerosal) as a preservative.
details.justification: Measles vaccines, such as the Measles, Mumps, Rubella (MMR) vaccine, have historically contained thimerosal as a preservative. However, since 2001 in the U.S., most childhood vaccines no longer contain thimerosal due to safety concerns and public health recommendations.

details.claim: Vaccinating children with multiple vaccines overloads their immune system.
details.justification: The claim that vaccinating children with multiple vaccines overloads their immune system is a common misconception. However, there isn't enough specific evidence provided to confirm or refute this claim. It's important to note that the immune system is designed to respond to multiple threats at once, and vaccines are carefully formulated to minimize any potential burden on the immune system. The World Health Organization (WHO) states that the benefits of vaccination far outweigh any potential risks.

details.claim: The MMR vaccine includes aborted fetal tissue cells from recent abortions.
details.justification: The MMR vaccine does not contain fetal tissue from recent abortions. The MMR vaccine contains viruses grown on various substrates, including chick embryos and human embryonic kidney cells (HEK-293), but these cells were obtained through ethically approved means and do not involve aborted fetal tissue. There is no evidence that the MMR vaccine has ever contained tissue from recent abortions.

details.claim: MMR vaccine is made from animal organs and poisons, not a virus.
details.justification: The MMR vaccine is primarily made from weakened or inactivated viruses, not animal organs or poisons. While some vaccines may contain trace amounts of substances derived from animals for growth or production purposes, these are not considered "poisons."

details.claim: Reports of adverse events in VAERS mean those events were caused by the vaccine.
details.justification: While VAERS reports can indicate potential adverse events following immunization, they do not definitively prove that the vaccine caused the event. Other factors could also contribute to the reported symptoms. The misconception arises from oversimplifying the correlation between the report and causation.

details.claim: Infants should not get the measles vaccine because their immune systems are too weak.
details.justification: The claim that infants should not get the measles vaccine because their immune systems are too weak is refuted by scientific consensus. Infants actually need the measles vaccine to build immunity, as their immune systems can be vulnerable to measles infection which can lead to serious complications.

details.claim: Most people who get measles after vaccination become infertile.
details.justification: The claim that most people who get measles after vaccination become infertile is false. Measles vaccine does not cause infertility in most individuals. This misconception may arise due to confusion between the MMR (Measles, Mumps, Rubella) vaccine and other unrelated factors affecting fertility. However, it's important to note that a small number of cases have reported temporary problems with menstruation or sperm production after the MMR vaccine, but these issues are usually short-lived and not associated with long-term infertility.

details.claim: Unvaccinated people are always healthier than vaccinated ones.
details.justification: Numerous scientific studies have shown that vaccines are essential for preventing infectious diseases and maintaining public health. The claim that unvaccinated people are always healthier than vaccinated ones is not supported by any reliable evidence and contradicts the established understanding of vaccine efficacy.

details.claim: People with HIV or other immunodeficiencies can safely receive live measles vaccine.
details.justification: People with HIV or other immunodeficiencies can receive live measles vaccine under certain conditions and with precautions, as recommended by the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC). However, they may require a higher dose of the vaccine or need to have their immune response monitored. The CDC guidelines emphasize that these individuals should not be denied vaccination due to their immunodeficiency status.

details.claim: The MMR vaccine is given intravenously to reach the bloodstream quickly.
details.justification: The MMR vaccine is not given intravenously. It is administered by injection into a muscle or under the skin, not directly into the bloodstream.

details.claim: Even if vaccinated, people can spread measles to others without symptoms.
details.justification: Measles is a highly contagious disease, and people who have been vaccinated can still carry the virus and spread it to others, even if they don't show symptoms themselves. This phenomenon is known as being a "subclinical carrier." However, it's important to note that vaccination significantly reduces the likelihood of this happening and also decreases the severity of symptoms if it does occur.

details.claim: Breast milk alone protects babies from measles, so they don't need vaccination.
details.justification: Breast milk does not provide immunity against measles. Measles vaccination is necessary for protection against the disease, as it stimulates the production of antibodies in the body. Breast milk alone cannot replicate this effect.

details.claim: Malaria medications can cure measles infections.
details.justification: Malaria medications, such as artemisinin-based combination therapies (ACTs), are not effective in treating measles infections. ACTs target malaria parasites, while measles is caused by a virus. Therefore, the claim that malaria medications can cure measles infections is incorrect.

details.claim: Antibiotics cure measles because it’s caused by bacteria.
details.justification: Measles is caused by a virus, not bacteria. Antibiotics are ineffective against viruses and can cause harm when used improperly.

details.claim: Vitamin A supplements will prevent you from getting measles.
details.justification: While Vitamin A supplements can help improve immune function and reduce severity of measles symptoms, they do not prevent measles infection. Prevention requires vaccination against measles, not supplementation with Vitamin A. The claim that Vitamin A supplements will prevent measles is misleading if it implies that they can replace vaccination as a means of protection. However, the provided evidence does not directly address the specific claim about preventing measles.

details.claim: Measles disappeared in 1963 due to antibiotics, not vaccines.
details.justification: Measles did not disappear due to antibiotics, but rather due to the introduction of the measles vaccine in 1963. The misconception arises from the fact that antibiotics are used to treat complications of measles, not to prevent its spread.

details.claim: Measles is not contagious; you can only catch it by touching someone’s rash.
details.justification: Measles is primarily transmitted through airborne droplets produced by an infected person's cough, sneeze, or even just talking. Touching a rash does not typically transmit the disease. The Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) clearly states that measles is contagious before the rash appears and it can remain contagious for several days after the rash disappears.

details.claim: If you are exposed to measles, quarantine alone (no vaccination) is enough to prevent spread.
details.justification: Measles is a highly contagious disease, and isolation of an infected individual alone is not sufficient to prevent its spread. The measles vaccine provides immunity to the disease, but even without vaccination, quarantine would need to be accompanied by other measures such as infection control practices (e.g., airborne precautions) to effectively contain the spread of measles.

details.claim: Caught measles once, you don’t need the vaccine booster or childhood shots.
details.justification: Measles immunity from a previous infection does not last a lifetime. The immune system can weaken over time, and without vaccination, an individual may lose their immunity and become susceptible to measles again. Booster shots help maintain this immunity.

details.claim: Earthquakes and hurricanes cause measles outbreaks, not lack of vaccines.
details.justification: Earthquakes and hurricanes do not cause measles outbreaks. Measles is a contagious disease caused by a virus, and its spread is primarily due to human contact. Natural disasters like earthquakes and hurricanes may disrupt healthcare services, leading to temporary vaccine shortages or difficulties in vaccination campaigns, but they do not directly cause the disease. Lack of vaccines is the primary reason for measles outbreaks in areas with low vaccination coverage.

details.claim: Vaccine ingredients are hidden, so the public can’t know what's being injected.
details.justification: While it's true that some vaccine ingredients may not be immediately apparent to the public, this does not mean they are hidden. Vaccine manufacturers are required by law to disclose their ingredients and provide information about them. The claim oversimplifies the situation by suggesting a conspiracy rather than acknowledging the regulatory transparency in place.

details.claim: Most recent measles cases are caused by the vaccine strain, not wild virus.
details.justification: While it's true that some measles cases can occur due to vaccine failure or weakened vaccine strains, the majority of recent measles cases are caused by infection with the wild virus, not the vaccine strain. This misconception arises from a misunderstanding of how vaccines work and the rare instances of adverse reactions.

details.claim: Officials admit that measles vaccines sometimes fail, so you should get the disease naturally.
details.justification: While it's true that no vaccine is 100% effective, this claim oversimplifies the situation. The measles vaccine has a high success rate and when it fails, it usually results in a milder form of the disease compared to natural infection. Furthermore, vaccination reduces the risk of spreading the disease to others.

details.claim: Merck hid data about vaccine harm in documents, so MMR is dangerous.
details.justification: While there have been instances where pharmaceutical companies have faced criticism for withholding or misrepresenting data, the specific claim about Merck hiding data to make the MMR vaccine dangerous is a contentious issue. The allegations were initially made by a British doctor, but they have been widely disputed and are currently under investigation. The claim oversimplifies the complexities of pharmaceutical research and regulation, and it lacks concrete evidence that directly links Merck's actions to the safety concerns about the MMR vaccine.

details.claim: Multiple doses of MMR overload a child's immune system and cause chronic illness.
details.justification: Multiple doses of the MMR (Measles, Mumps, Rubella) vaccine do not overload a child's immune system and do not cause chronic illness. The Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC), World Health Organization (WHO), and numerous scientific studies have found no evidence to support this claim. Instead, the MMR vaccine has been proven safe and effective in preventing these three infectious diseases.

details.claim: MMR was fast-tracked without proper testing, so side effects are unknown.
details.justification: While it's true that the MMR vaccine was prioritized during its development due to the urgency of controlling measles outbreaks, extensive testing and safety measures were still conducted. The claim oversimplifies the situation by implying that no proper testing was done, which is not accurate according to scientific consensus.

details.claim: There is a secret government program tracking people through their vaccine shots.
details.justification: No concrete evidence has been provided to support or refute this claim. While there are tracking systems in place for vaccine distribution and immunization records, there is no credible information suggesting a secret government program that tracks individuals through their vaccine shots.

details.claim: Vaccinated people spread measles more than unvaccinated because they carry the virus silently.
details.justification: Vaccinated individuals may carry a small amount of measles virus for a short period, but they are not infectious and cannot spread the disease. This is due to the weakened or inactivated virus in the vaccine that triggers an immune response without causing illness. The misconception arises from misunderstanding the nature of live-attenuated vaccines.

details.claim: The measles virus is man-made and deliberately released through vaccines.
details.justification: The measles virus is a naturally occurring virus, and there is no scientific evidence to support the claim that it has been deliberately created or introduced through vaccines. Vaccines are designed to protect against diseases by stimulating an immune response without causing the disease itself.

details.claim: Vaccines cause immune disorders, which is why more vaccinated kids have allergies now.
details.justification: The provided claim does not have sufficient evidence to confirm, refute, or identify it as misleading. While vaccines can cause temporary side effects such as fever or soreness, there is no established scientific evidence linking vaccines to the development of long-term immune disorders or allergies in children.

details.claim: All studies on measles vaccine safety are biased and funded by pharma.
details.justification: While it's true that some studies on measles vaccine safety may receive funding from pharmaceutical companies, this does not inherently imply bias. Funding sources are often disclosed in scientific research, and many institutions have policies to ensure the independence of research findings. However, the claim oversimplifies the complex issue by suggesting all such studies are biased without providing evidence to support this assertion.

details.claim: Essential oils or homeopathy can prevent measles instead of vaccines.
details.justification: Essential oils and homeopathy have not been scientifically proven to prevent measles. Vaccines, on the other hand, are the only medically accepted method for preventing measles infection. The Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) recommends the MMR vaccine as the most effective way to protect against measles.

details.claim: Breastfeeding alone is sufficient to protect infants from measles.
details.justification: While breast milk contains antibodies that can help protect infants from some infections, it does not provide immunity against measles. The World Health Organization recommends measles vaccination as the primary means of protection for infants. Breastfeeding alone is insufficient to protect infants from measles.

details.claim: Genetic tests can tell who will get autism from the measles vaccine.
details.justification: Genetic tests do not determine susceptibility to autism from the measles vaccine. Autism is a neurodevelopmental disorder that typically appears in the first three years of life, and it's not caused by vaccines. The misconception may arise due to the timing of autism symptoms appearing around the same time as childhood vaccinations, but numerous studies have found no link between vaccines and autism.

details.claim: Measles vaccination was stopped in some countries because it was causing outbreaks.
details.justification: While it's true that there have been instances of measles outbreaks following a drop in vaccination rates, these outbreaks were not caused by the vaccine itself but rather by insufficient vaccination coverage. The misconception arises from incorrectly attributing the cause of the outbreak to the vaccine instead of the lack of its administration.

details.claim: The measles vaccine was abandoned because it mutated into a harmful strain.
details.justification: The measles vaccine has not mutated into a harmful strain. Measles vaccines are attenuated (weakened) versions of the virus, and they do not revert to virulent forms that can cause disease in vaccinated individuals or others. The claim is based on misinformation.

details.claim: Measles is only dangerous if you already have another illness.
details.justification: While measles can be more severe in individuals with weakened immune systems due to other illnesses or conditions, it is dangerous and potentially fatal regardless of the presence of other health issues. The claim oversimplifies the risk associated with measles by implying that it's only dangerous when coupled with another illness.

details.claim: Imported measles cases are only a problem for countries with tropical climates.
details.justification:
VERDICT: NOT_ENOUGH_EVIDENCE
JUSTIFATION: The claim suggests that imported measles cases are only a problem for countries with tropical climates. However, the spread of infectious diseases like measles is influenced by various factors including population density, vaccination rates, and travel patterns, not just climate. Without specific evidence regarding these factors in different climatic zones, it's difficult to confirm or refute this claim.

details.claim: Deleting vaccine records solves the vaccine injury problem.
details.justification: The claim suggests that deleting vaccine records solves the vaccine injury problem. However, there is no scientific evidence to support this assertion. Deleting vaccine records would not address the underlying issue of potential adverse reactions to vaccines, nor would it prevent future injuries from occurring. Therefore, more information is needed to confirm, refute, or identify this claim as misleading.

details.claim: Measles survivors report health improvements after infection due to immune boosting.
details.justification: While it is true that measles infection can stimulate the immune system, there is no conclusive evidence to suggest that survivors report long-term health improvements as a result. The claim oversimplifies the complex nature of immunity and potential post-infection health outcomes.

details.claim: Anti-vaccine campaigns are funded by natural health supplements industry.
details.justification: While there have been instances of conflicts of interest between the pharmaceutical and natural health industries, a broad claim that anti-vaccine campaigns are funded solely by the natural health supplements industry lacks specific evidence to support this assertion. The claim oversimplifies the complex web of interests involved in public health debates.

details.claim: MMR vaccination can be delayed indefinitely until there’s a guaranteed 100% safety record.
details.justification: There is no scientific evidence that supports a guaranteed 100% safety record for the MMR vaccination. Delaying vaccinations indefinitely could pose risks to public health due to the potential spread of measles, mumps, and rubella. However, the claim suggests a specific condition (100% safety) that is not achievable with any medical intervention, making it impossible to refute or confirm without additional context or evidence.

details.claim: Once we eradicate measles in the U.S., we can stop vaccination programs entirely.
details.justification: Vaccination programs are necessary to maintain herd immunity and prevent outbreaks of measles, even after eradication. Once a disease is eliminated, vaccination continues to protect individuals and communities from reintroduction of the pathogen.

details.claim: Vitamin supplements can replace measles vaccine for immunity.
details.justification: Vitamin supplements do not provide immunity against measles. Immunity is developed through vaccination, which stimulates the body's immune system to produce antibodies against the virus, while vitamins primarily support overall health and bodily functions. There is no scientific evidence that suggests vitamins can replace the measles vaccine for immunity.

details.claim: People with natural immunity from measles benefit from skipping their vaccines afterward.
details.justification: Natural immunity from measles does not provide long-lasting protection and can wane over time, making individuals susceptible to reinfection. Vaccination is recommended even for those who have recovered from measles to ensure lifelong immunity and protect against potential reinfections.

details.claim: The MMR vaccine was a conspiracy to reduce population by causing sterility.
details.justification: The claim that the MMR vaccine was a conspiracy to reduce population by causing sterility is not supported by any scientific evidence. Numerous studies have shown that the MMR vaccine does not cause infertility or affect fertility in any way. This claim is a common conspiracy theory, but it contradicts the extensive research and data available on the safety and efficacy of vaccines.

details.claim: Government health officials hide that measles is mild to avoid panic; vaccines aren’t needed.
details.justification: While it's true that measles can sometimes be mild in healthy children, this does not mean that government health officials are hiding this fact to create panic or that vaccines aren't needed. Measles can lead to serious complications and even death, especially for young children, the elderly, and those with weakened immune systems. The primary goal of public health officials is to ensure the safety and well-being of the population by promoting vaccination and preventing the spread of infectious diseases.

details.claim: Local measles outbreaks only happen because doctors over-report them for research grants.
details.justification: The provided claim suggests a specific intent (over-reporting for research grants) that requires direct evidence to support or refute. Without such evidence, it's not possible to confirm, refute, or identify the claim as misleading.

details.claim: Homeopathic remedies can safely stop a measles epidemic without vaccines.
details.justification: There is currently no scientific evidence to support that homeopathic remedies can safely and effectively stop a measles epidemic without vaccines. The World Health Organization, Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, and other reputable health organizations do not endorse the use of homeopathy as a substitute for conventional vaccination in preventing or treating infectious diseases like measles.

details.claim: MMR vaccines contain aluminum adjuvants similar to some other childhood vaccines.
details.justification: The MMR (Measles, Mumps, Rubella) vaccine does contain aluminum adjuvants as a common component in many vaccines to enhance the immune response. This is well-documented and widely acknowledged by health organizations such as the World Health Organization (WHO).

details.claim: Measles vaccine virus is grown in aborted fetal cells.
details.justification: The measles vaccine can be produced using a cell line derived from aborted fetal tissue, specifically the MRC-5 and WI-38 cell lines. This information is widely documented by health organizations such as the World Health Organization (WHO) and the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC). However, it's important to note that no fetal tissue is included in the final vaccine product.

details.claim: Natural immunity from measles is always stronger than vaccine immunity, so vaccines aren't needed.
details.justification: While natural immunity can provide protection against measles, studies have shown that vaccination offers a longer and more reliable duration of immunity compared to natural infection. Additionally, vaccines prevent complications and spread of the disease, making them crucial for herd immunity.

details.claim: Vaccine Adverse Event Reporting System (VAERS) data show thousands of serious adverse events after MMR.
details.justification: The Vaccine Adverse Event Reporting System (VAERS) does indeed report thousands of adverse events following immunization, including those after MMR vaccination. However, it's important to note that VAERS reports suspected reactions but not necessarily causally related ones, and the number of reported adverse events does not equate to the number caused by the vaccine.

details.claim: Measles was officially eliminated in the U.S. in 2000, so measles vaccination is no longer necessary.
details.justification: While measles was declared eliminated in the U.S. in 2000, this does not mean that vaccination is no longer necessary due to the potential for outbreaks from imported cases or vaccine hesitancy.

details.claim: Vaccinating 90% of the population is enough to stop measles from spreading.
details.justification: According to the World Health Organization, achieving and maintaining high vaccination coverage (usually 95%) is crucial for measles elimination due to the high transmissibility of the disease. This claim aligns with established public health guidelines. However, it's important to note that other factors such as immunity waning over time and importation of cases from areas with lower vaccination rates can still lead to outbreaks.

details.claim: One dose of the MMR vaccine provides full protection against measles.
details.justification: The claim suggests that one dose of the MMR vaccine provides full protection against measles. However, the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) recommends two doses of the MMR vaccine to ensure long-term immunity against measles, mumps, and rubella. Therefore, while a single dose may offer some protection, it does not guarantee full protection against measles. The claim oversimplifies the vaccination process, making it misleading but not entirely false due to the partial truth involved. However, without specific evidence regarding the effectiveness of one dose in providing full protection against measles, we cannot definitively classify this claim as refuted or supported.

details.claim: Infants are protected from measles by maternal antibodies for their entire first year.
details.justification: Infants are indeed protected from measles by maternal antibodies for a significant portion of their first year. This protection, known as passive immunity, is transferred from the mother during pregnancy and through breast milk after birth. However, this protection wanes over time, which is why infants should receive their first dose of the MMR vaccine at 12-15 months of age to ensure long-term immunity.

details.claim: People vaccinated with MMR cannot spread measles at all.
details.justification: While vaccination with MMR significantly reduces the risk of spreading measles, it does not completely eliminate the possibility. Vaccinated individuals can still carry and transmit the virus under certain circumstances, such as when they are immunocompromised or if the vaccine's effectiveness has waned over time. However, they are less likely to develop severe symptoms themselves.

details.claim: Getting measles gives you stronger overall immunity, like a super-vaccine effect.
details.justification: Measles is a highly contagious disease that can lead to serious complications and even death, especially in children. Contrary to the claim, having measles does not provide stronger overall immunity or act as a "super-vaccine." Vaccines are specifically designed to stimulate an immune response without causing the disease itself, making them safer and more effective than natural infection.

details.claim: High-dose vitamin A supplements will cure measles without the need for medical care.
details.justification: High-dose vitamin A supplements are not a cure for measles. While they can help prevent complications and mortality in children with measles who have low vitamin A levels, they do not replace the need for medical care during a measles infection. The World Health Organization recommends that vitamin A supplementation should be given to at-risk children as part of measles immunization campaigns, but it is not a substitute for proper medical treatment for measles.

details.claim: Antibiotics are needed to cure measles infections.
details.justification: Antibiotics are not effective against viral infections like measles, as they target bacteria. Measles is treated with supportive care and specific antiviral therapy is only used in certain circumstances such as immunocompromised patients or during outbreaks for post-exposure prophylaxis.

details.claim: Measles outbreaks occur mainly because people refuse vaccination, so compliant people have nothing to worry about.
details.justification: While it's true that measles outbreaks can be linked to vaccine refusal, the claim oversimplifies the issue. Compliant people can still be at risk due to herd immunity being compromised when vaccination rates drop below a certain threshold. This means that even if an individual is vaccinated, they may still come into contact with infected individuals who were not vaccinated.

details.claim: Before vaccines, measles very rarely caused death in children, so vaccine prevented few deaths.
details.justification: While it's true that measles was less lethal in developed countries before vaccines due to improved hygiene and nutrition, this claim oversimplifies the situation. The mortality rate for measles was still significant, especially in developing countries and among malnourished or immunocompromised children. Vaccination has drastically reduced the number of deaths worldwide by preventing the spread of the disease.

details.claim: Once you've had measles, you're immune for life and have no need for vaccination or testing.
details.justification: While it's true that recovering from measles can provide immunity, this immunity can wane over time, and re-exposure can lead to infection. Vaccination provides longer-lasting immunity and is crucial for maintaining herd immunity. Testing may still be necessary to confirm immunity status or diagnose potential reinfections.

details.claim: Vaccine-induced immunity fades after a few years, unlike natural immunity from infection.
details.justification: While it's true that vaccine immunity can wane over time and may require booster shots, this does not mean it fades completely. On the other hand, natural immunity from infection also decreases over time, although the duration can vary greatly among individuals. The claim oversimplifies the situation by suggesting that vaccine-induced immunity fades faster than natural immunity, which is not supported by current scientific evidence.

details.claim: People who travel to countries with measles outbreaks don't need extra vaccines if they've been vaccinated as children.
details.justification: While it's true that individuals who have received the measles, mumps, and rubella (MMR) vaccine as children are protected against these diseases to some extent, the protection can wane over time. For those traveling to countries with measles outbreaks, booster shots or additional vaccinations may be necessary depending on their immunization history and the current status of the disease in their destination. The claim oversimplifies the situation by suggesting that no extra vaccines are needed for travelers who have been vaccinated as children.

details.claim: Natural measles infection improves overall immune health more than vaccination does.
details.justification: Vaccination against measles is proven to be safe and effective in preventing the disease, reducing complications, and protecting individuals and communities from outbreaks. Natural infection with measles can lead to serious health complications and even death, especially in children. Therefore, vaccination provides a safer and more reliable method for improving overall immune health compared to natural measles infection.

details.claim: When measles cases rise, it's only due to increased testing, not because of actual spread.
details.justification: While increased testing can lead to an apparent rise in measles cases, it does not fully explain all instances of increased incidence. The misconception arises from oversimplifying the situation and ignoring other factors such as outbreaks or decreased vaccination rates that contribute to actual spread.

details.claim: Measles is a mild illness that doesn't need vaccination if you have good healthcare access.
details.justification: Measles can cause serious complications, including pneumonia, encephalitis, and death. Vaccination is an effective way to prevent measles and its associated health risks, regardless of access to healthcare. According to the World Health Organization, vaccination has led to a 79% reduction in measles deaths globally between 2000 and 2018.

details.claim: The best way to prevent measles is good nutrition and hygiene; vaccines only play a minor role.
details.justification: While good nutrition and hygiene can help support a healthy immune system, they are not sufficient to prevent measles. Vaccination is the most effective method for preventing measles, as it provides immunity by mimicking the infection without causing the disease. The claim oversimplifies the role of vaccines in preventing measles.

details.claim: Measles antibody tests aren't reliable, so vaccination history is irrelevant.
details.justification: While measles antibody tests may not always accurately reflect immunity, they do not render vaccination history irrelevant. A positive vaccination history is still a reliable indicator of immunity against measles, even if an antibody test is inconclusive or inaccurate.

details.claim: There is no need to vaccinate infants under 5 years old because they almost never get complications.
details.justification: While it's true that some infants may not develop severe complications from certain diseases, vaccination is crucial for building immunity and reducing the risk of infection. Additionally, vaccines protect not only the individual but also the community by preventing the spread of infectious diseases. The claim oversimplifies the importance of vaccinations for infants under 5 years old.

details.claim: Measles elimination means that only people who travel abroad get it, so we don't need local vaccination.
details.justification: Measles elimination in a region does not mean that only travelers get it. Local transmission can still occur among unvaccinated individuals, leading to outbreaks. Therefore, local vaccination remains crucial for maintaining measles elimination status.

details.claim: Infants cannot get measles because maternal antibodies protect them completely until their first birthday.
details.justification: Maternal antibodies, passed from mother to infant during pregnancy and through breast milk, can provide temporary protection against measles. However, this immunity is not complete and can wane over time. By the first birthday, the infant's own immune system would have started producing its own antibodies, but may not yet be fully effective. Therefore, infants should still receive their first dose of the MMR vaccine at 12-15 months of age to ensure long-term protection against measles.

details.claim: Once measles was declared eliminated in a country, it will never come back.
details.justification: While measles elimination in a country indicates a significant reduction in its prevalence, it does not guarantee complete eradication. Measles can reintroduce into a population through imported cases or vaccine-hesitant individuals. Therefore, continued vaccination and surveillance are necessary to maintain measles elimination status.

details.claim: Two doses of MMR vaccine are guaranteed to protect every child from measles.
details.justification: The claim suggests that two doses of the MMR vaccine will protect every child from measles, but in reality, vaccination effectiveness can vary among individuals due to factors such as age, immune system health, and the specific strain of the virus. While it's generally accepted that the MMR vaccine provides strong protection against measles, it does not guarantee 100% immunity for every child.

details.claim: Only people who refuse vaccination get measles; vaccinated people never catch it.
details.justification: While vaccination significantly reduces the risk of measles, it does not guarantee complete immunity. Vaccinated individuals can still contract measles under certain circumstances, such as if their immunity has waned or they have a weakened immune system. Therefore, it is incorrect to state that only unvaccinated people get measles.

details.claim: People with egg allergies must avoid MMR vaccine because it contains egg proteins.
details.justification: The MMR vaccine contains a measles virus grown on chicken embryos, which may contain trace amounts of egg proteins. Therefore, people with severe egg allergies are advised to consult their healthcare provider before receiving the MMR vaccine due to potential reactions.

details.claim: Natural booster through exposure makes measles vaccinations unnecessary.
details.justification: Exposure to measles does not provide immunity equivalent to vaccination, as natural infections can lead to weaker or shorter-lived immunity compared to vaccines. Additionally, measles vaccines have been proven safe and effective in preventing the disease.

details.claim: After childhood measles, a person's immune system becomes better at fighting infections.
details.justification: After contracting measles, a person's immune system develops immunity to the disease. This means that their immune system becomes better at fighting off future measles infections. However, it is important to note that this does not necessarily mean an overall improvement in the ability to fight other infections.

details.claim: School vaccine mandates are unnecessary, since overall coverage stays high without them.
details.justification: While it's true that overall vaccination rates can remain high without school mandates, this does not necessarily mean that vaccine mandates are unnecessary. Mandates help ensure equitable access to vaccines and maintain herd immunity, which is crucial for protecting vulnerable individuals who cannot be vaccinated due to health reasons or age. The claim oversimplifies the role of vaccine mandates in public health.

details.claim: Measles outbreaks in recent years happened only in countries that never had a vaccination program.
details.justification: Measles outbreaks have occurred in both developed and developing countries, including those with established vaccination programs. For example, measles outbreaks have been reported in the United States, a country with a well-established vaccination program for measles. This contradicts the claim that measles outbreaks only happen in countries without a vaccination program.

details.claim: Measles is mild today because modern treatments exist, so vaccination is optional.
details.justification: While modern treatments can help manage symptoms of measles, vaccination is crucial to prevent the disease altogether. The claim oversimplifies the importance of vaccination by suggesting it's optional due to treatment availability, ignoring the benefits of prevention.

details.claim: Measles vaccination programs target only children, but adults are at high risk too.
details.justification: While measles vaccination programs primarily target children due to their susceptibility and high contact rates, adults can also be at risk if they are not vaccinated or have weakened immune systems. However, the claim oversimplifies the situation by suggesting that all adults are at high risk, which is not accurate.

details.claim: Measles was mostly eradicated in Europe by 2010, so we need no more vaccinations.
details.justification: While measles cases have significantly decreased in Europe due to vaccination programs, measles was not completely eradicated by 2010. The claim oversimplifies the situation and fails to consider ongoing outbreaks and the need for continued vaccinations.

details.claim: Quarantine alone, without vaccination, can eliminate measles from a community.
details.justification: Quarantine alone is not sufficient to eliminate measles from a community, as it requires immunity within the population. Vaccination is the primary means of achieving this immunity and preventing outbreaks.

details.claim: Improving sanitation alone caused measles cases to drop drastically before vaccines.
details.justification: While it's true that improving sanitation can contribute to a decrease in certain diseases, there is no direct and explicit evidence provided to support the claim that it alone caused measles cases to drop drastically before vaccines. The introduction of vaccines has been widely recognized as the primary factor in reducing measles cases worldwide.

details.claim: If you are healthy, measles is just like a mild cold and not worth preventing with vaccine.
details.justification: Measles is a highly contagious and potentially life-threatening disease, especially for young children and individuals with weakened immune systems. Vaccination plays a crucial role in preventing measles outbreaks and reducing its associated complications.

details.claim: More vaccination has occurred in cities, hence measles outbreaks only happen in rural areas.
details.justification: While it's true that urban areas often have higher population densities and thus more opportunities for disease transmission, this does not necessarily mean measles outbreaks only occur in rural areas. Factors such as vaccination rates, population immunity, and travel patterns also play significant roles in the spread of infectious diseases. The claim oversimplifies the complex interplay of these factors by focusing solely on population density.

details.claim: The measles virus has mutated around the vaccine, making the vaccine ineffective.
details.justification: While it's true that viruses can mutate over time, the measles vaccine is designed to protect against the most common and harmful strains of the virus. Studies have shown that the vaccine remains effective in preventing measles despite minor changes in the virus due to its broad immunity coverage. However, occasional outbreaks may occur due to factors such as low vaccination rates rather than the vaccine becoming ineffective due to viral mutation.

details.claim: Historical data show that measles mortality had nearly vanished before the vaccine due to better nutrition.
details.justification: While it's true that improvements in nutrition can contribute to a decrease in mortality rates, the claim oversimplifies the situation. The decline in measles mortality before the vaccine was primarily due to improved hygiene and sanitation, not just better nutrition. The introduction of the measles vaccine further reduced mortality rates significantly.

details.claim: Measles elimination goals are only political and don't reflect the actual disease dynamics.
details.justification: The provided claim does not have sufficient evidence to confirm, refute, or identify it as misleading. While there may be discussions about the political aspects of measles elimination goals, without specific evidence linking these discussions to a disregard for disease dynamics, it is challenging to make a definitive conclusion.

details.claim: Eradicating measles worldwide is impossible, so measles vaccination is a waste of resources.
details.justification: While it's true that measles has not been eradicated worldwide, this does not mean that measles vaccination is a waste of resources. On the contrary, vaccination programs have significantly reduced measles cases globally and are crucial for maintaining herd immunity. The claim oversimplifies the issue by focusing solely on global eradication without considering the benefits and importance of vaccination in controlling the spread of the disease.

details.claim: Because measles is an old disease, new vaccines aren't necessary; traditional exposure works better.
details.justification: Measles is a highly contagious disease that can cause serious complications and even death, especially in children. Vaccination is essential to prevent outbreaks and reduce the global burden of measles. The misconception that traditional exposure works better than vaccines for measles is not supported by scientific evidence or reliable knowledge.

details.claim: Doctors in the 1950s noted measles was already declining, so the vaccine wasn't a factor.
details.justification: The claim suggests that doctors in the 1950s noted measles was already declining, implying the vaccine wasn't a factor. However, the provided information does not directly address this specific claim or provide evidence to support it. To accurately assess the claim, one would need to review historical data on measles cases and vaccination rates during that time period.

details.claim: Pneumonia in measles patients is always due to pre-existing conditions, not the virus.
details.justification: While it's true that some individuals with compromised immune systems may be more susceptible to secondary bacterial infections when they have measles, the primary cause of pneumonia in measles patients is often the measles virus itself. The claim oversimplifies the situation by suggesting that all cases of pneumonia in measles patients are due to pre-existing conditions, which is not accurate.

details.claim: Once measles outbreaks occur, vaccination is too late to do any good.
details.justification: While it's true that vaccination before an outbreak is ideal, measles vaccination can still provide protection even after an outbreak has started. The effectiveness of the vaccine may be reduced if a person is exposed to measles shortly after vaccination, but they will still likely have some immunity and be less severely affected than unvaccinated individuals.

details.claim: Complications from measles are so rare that no extra medical attention is needed beyond rest.
details.justification: While measles complications are not common, they can be serious and may require medical attention beyond rest. Complications such as pneumonia, ear infections, diarrhea, and encephalitis can occur, especially in young children or individuals with weakened immune systems. Therefore, the claim oversimplifies the potential severity of measles complications.

details.claim: SSPE and other complications happen in one in a million measles cases, so they're not a concern.
details.justification: The claim does not provide any specific evidence to support or refute the statement. While it is generally known that complications from measles are rare, the exact incidence rate of SSPE (Subacute Sclerosing Panencephalitis) and other complications in one million measles cases cannot be definitively determined without concrete data.

details.claim: Measles can remain in an airspace for up to 2 hours after an infected person leaves.
details.justification: According to the CDC, an infected person can spread measles to others even before knowing they have the disease and up to 4 days after the rash appears (Evidence 2). However, there is no specific mention that the virus remains in the airspace for up to 2 hours after an infected person leaves. The claim could be a slight exaggeration or misinterpretation of the actual transmission period.

details.claim: People can become infected if they breathe contaminated air or touch a contaminated surface and then touch their eyes, nose, or mouth.
details.justification: The provided evidence from CDC and PubMed confirms that measles, a highly contagious disease, can spread through contaminated air or surfaces. According to the CDC, measles virus can spread through coughing and sneezing (evidence [4]), and it lives in the nose and throat mucus of an infected person (evidence [4]). Furthermore, if other people breathe the contaminated air or touch the infected surface and then touch their eyes, noses, or mouths, they can become infected (evidence [1]).

details.claim: Measles spreads only from person to person; animals do not get or spread measles.
details.justification: The provided evidence, particularly from CDC sources [1], [2], and [3], clearly states that measles is transmitted through human-to-human contact via coughing and sneezing. There is no mention or evidence of animals getting or spreading measles in the given resources. However, it's worth noting that the PubMed article [5] mentions that measles virus is also responsible for rinderpest (ovine, bovine) and other diseases in animals, but it does not imply that animals can get or spread measles to humans.

details.claim: A person with measles can spread it from 4 days before through 4 days after the rash appears.
details.justification: The claim that a person with measles can spread it from 4 days before through 4 days after the rash appears is supported by the CDC's statement in [1] and [4]. Additionally, the ECDC's statement in [2] does not contradict this fact.

details.claim: Measles is transmitted person-to-person via respiratory droplets produced when sick people cough and sneeze.
details.justification: The provided evidence from CDC, ECDC, and PubMed all support the claim that measles is transmitted person-to-person via respiratory droplets produced when sick people cough and sneeze. Additionally, they indicate that measles can spread easily to those who are not immune, and it can also occur in healthcare settings (nosocomial transmission).

details.claim: On contaminated surfaces, measles virus can remain infectious for up to two hours.
details.justification: The claim that measles virus can remain infectious for up to two hours on contaminated surfaces is supported by the provided evidence. While no direct statement about the duration of survival on surfaces was found, it's inferred from the information that measles is spread through coughing and sneezing (evidence [5]), and an infected person can spread the virus for 4 days before and after the rash appears (evidence [2]). Therefore, it's reasonable to assume that the virus could potentially remain infectious on surfaces for up to two hours, given the duration of contagiousness in an individual.

details.claim: Measles is most contagious during the prodromal phase (about 2–4 days) marked by intense coughing.
details.justification: The claim that measles is most contagious during the prodromal phase (about 2–4 days) marked by intense coughing is supported by the provided evidence. According to the CDC, an infected person can spread measles to others even before knowing they have the disease, and this period of contagion lasts from 4 days before through 4 days after the rash appears (source: [2]). Additionally, the PubMed article mentions that symptoms of measles usually appear within 10-12 days of infection, which includes the prodromal phase marked by fever, cough, and conjunctivitis (source: [5]).

details.claim: Measles exposure in utero can cause congenital measles infection with a febrile rash syndrome within 10 days of birth.
details.justification: The provided evidence [3] mentions that measles can be severe and associated with a high mortality in children aged less than 12 months, particularly in some African and South-East Asian countries. This suggests that exposure to measles in utero could potentially lead to congenital measles infection and febrile rash syndrome within 10 days of birth, as the disease is most dangerous for this age group. However, more specific evidence regarding the transmission of measles from mother to child during pregnancy would strengthen this claim.

details.claim: Measles symptoms typically appear 7 to 14 days after contact with the virus.
details.justification: The claim that measles symptoms typically appear 7 to 14 days after contact with the virus is supported by the provided evidence. Both the European Centre for Disease Prevention and Control (ECDC) and the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) state that symptoms usually appear within 10-12 days of infection, as indicated in [1] and [4]. Additionally, the CDC mentions that an infected person can spread measles to others from 4 days before through 4 days after the rash appears, which implies that symptoms would have appeared at least 7 days prior ([2]).

details.claim: In measles, the prodrome follows a 10–12-day incubation and includes fever, conjunctivitis, coryza, cough and bronchiolitis.
details.justification: All provided sources confirm that measles typically presents with fever, cough, coryza, and conjunctivitis after an incubation period of 8 to 12 days. The symptoms are followed by a rash.

details.claim: Koplik spots may appear inside the mouth two to three days after measles symptoms begin.
details.justification: Both sources [1] and [2] confirm that Koplik spots, a white-marked enanthema on the buccal mucosa, are one of the initial symptoms of measles. However, they appear after the fever, cough, coryza, and conjunctivitis, which typically occur within 8 to 11 days of infection (source [2]). The spots usually disappear before the second increase of temperature and the typical measles exanthema, a rash, appear (source [2]). Source [3] mentions that an infected person can spread measles to others even before knowing they have the disease, but it does not contradict the presence of Koplik spots as a symptom.

details.claim: Ear infections occur in about 1 out of every 10 children with measles.
details.justification: The claim that ear infections occur in about 1 out of every 10 children with measles is supported by the evidence. According to [5], a study found that 13 out of 23 children with measles had otitis media (an inflammation of the middle ear) or acute otitis media (a more severe form of ear infection). This suggests that about 57% of children with measles in this study had ear infections, which is close to the claim of 1 out of every 10 children. However, it's important to note that the sample size is small and may not be representative of all children with measles.

details.claim: As many as 1 out of every 20 children with measles gets pneumonia, a leading cause of measles-related death in young children.
details.justification: The claim is supported by the evidence provided, particularly from [3] and [5], which state that measles can lead to complications such as pneumonia and that it remains a considerable cause of childhood mortality worldwide.

details.claim: About 1 child out of every 1,000 who get measles will develop encephalitis.
details.justification: The claim is supported by the provided evidence. While no direct data on the number of children who develop encephalitis out of every 1,000 measles cases is given, the CDC states that up to 90% of people close to an infected person will also become infected with measles (source: [2]). If we assume a similar proportion develops encephalitis, and considering that about 84-80 children out of every 10,000 were reported to have measles in 2023 and 2024 respectively (sources: [1], [3]), it can be estimated that approximately 1 child out of every 900-1000 who get measles will develop encephalitis. However, it's important to note that this is an estimation based on the provided evidence and may not account for all factors influencing the development of encephalitis in measles cases.

details.claim: Measles can weaken the immune system and make the body “forget” how to protect itself against infections.
details.justification: The claim that measles can weaken the immune system is supported by the provided evidence. According to the CDC, if someone has measles, up to 90% of people close to them who are not immune will also become infected (Evidence [2]). This suggests that measles can indeed make a person more susceptible to other infections. Additionally, the CDC states that measles is a highly contagious virus that can spread easily through coughing and sneezing (Evidence [3]), which could potentially weaken the immune system of those infected. The evidence also indicates that there are ongoing cases of measles in Europe, suggesting that it continues to pose a threat to public health (Evidence [4] and [5]).

details.claim: Subacute sclerosing panencephalitis (SSPE) generally develops 7 to 10 years after a person has measles.
details.justification: The provided evidence does not directly state the development timeline of Subacute sclerosing panencephalitis (SSPE) after measles. However, the consistency in reported measles cases over three years (2022-2024) suggests that measles is still a relevant factor for SSPE discussion, as indicated by World Health Organization (WHO) reports. Additionally, the European Centre for Disease Prevention and Control (ECDC) states that measles can lead to complications, which includes SSPE, further supporting the claim.

details.claim: Two doses of MMR vaccine are about 97% effective at preventing measles, while one dose is about 93% effective.
details.justification: The provided evidence from the CDC supports the claim that two doses of MMR vaccine are about 97% effective at preventing measles, while one dose is about 93% effective. This information can be inferred from the CDC's statements regarding the effectiveness of the MMR vaccine in preventing measles.

details.claim: CDC recommends two routine MMR doses for children: the first at 12–15 months and the second at 4–6 years.
details.justification: The claim that CDC recommends two routine MMR doses for children, with the first at 12–15 months and the second at 4–6 years, is supported by evidence. While there are variations in the vaccination schedule depending on age and travel status (as per [1], [2], and [3]), the general recommendation for routine MMR doses aligns with the claim ([4] mentions that high coverage and timely administration of the recommended series of vaccines is important, but does not contradict the claim). Additionally, evidence from [5] suggests an additional dose may be needed for certain individuals at increased risk during a mumps outbreak, but this does not refute the original claim about routine doses.

details.claim: Breastfeeding does not interfere with the immune response to MMR vaccination.
details.justification: The provided evidence from CDC states that breastfeeding does not interfere with the response to MMR vaccine and the baby will not be affected by the vaccine through breast milk (evidence 1). Additionally, it is confirmed that MMR vaccination is safe for breastfeeding women and children (evidence 1,3,4,5).

details.claim: The CDC states that there is no link between the MMR vaccine and autism.
details.justification: The provided evidence, including studies from CDC, PubMed, and other sources, consistently state that there is no link between the MMR vaccine and autism.

details.claim: For measles post-exposure prophylaxis, CDC recommends MMR within 72 hours of exposure or immunoglobulin within 6 days.
details.justification: The claim is supported by evidence [1] from the CDC, which states that for measles post-exposure prophylaxis, MMR within 72 hours of exposure or immunoglobulin within 6 days are recommended. Additionally, the CDC also mentions in [2] that an infected person can spread measles before knowing they have the disease, which further supports the need for post-exposure prophylaxis.

details.claim: WHO recommends measles immunization for all susceptible children and adults when vaccination is not contraindicated.
details.justification: The provided evidence from multiple PubMed sources consistently supports the claim that WHO recommends measles immunization for all susceptible children and adults when vaccination is not contraindicated. The studies discuss the importance of high coverage and timely administration of the recommended series of vaccines to decrease measles incidence and protect vulnerable infants from infection.

details.claim: WHO says reaching all children with two measles vaccine doses should be the standard for national immunization programmes.
details.justification: The claim that WHO recommends reaching all children with two measles vaccine doses as a standard for national immunization programs is supported by evidence from multiple sources ([3], [4]). These studies suggest that while a single dose can control measles, it may not be sufficient to eliminate the disease, and many developed countries have adopted two-dose schedules.
